text: <par>Lalo <latinform>me²¹</latinform> is distinct from the morpheme for 'eye', <latinform>ʔmI̱³³</latinform>.</par>

<par>Note the similarity between the last two syllables and the Lahu forms <latinform>ɔ̀-ku-câʔ</latinform> 'vein, sinew, tendon' and <latinform>ɔ̀-ku-ɔ̀-câʔ</latinform> 'every bone in the body'.</par>
<par>M2: 1390? (or do all these with <latinform>-e/</latinform>etc. come from another Ety. and not 1390: perh. one with an -<latinform>r</latinform>?)</par>
<par>This form seems to be shuttled back and forth between ety 71 and 80.</par>
<par>Is 2d syll suffix or e17 (cf. Sani forms)?</par>

<par>The syllabic nasals in the Nesu and Noesu compounds are possibly developments from a previous <latinform>w-</latinform> or zero initial. A similar, though opposite development can be seen in the correspondence of Cantonese initial <latinform>ŋ-</latinform> and Mandarin <latinform>w-</latinform> (e.g. Cantonese 'five' and 1st.pro vs. Mandarin 'five' and 1st.pro).</par>
<par>2d syll tagged variously as 387, and as 2005.</par>

<par>We are assuming dialectal variation between initial <latinform>l-</latinform> and <latinform>n-</latinform> in Nesu, a phenomenon also characteristic of Southwest Mandarin and other languages in the area.</par>
<par>The second element in the Nesu and Noesu compounds < <xref ref="1013">#1013</xref>.</par>

<par>The syllabic nasals in the Nesu and Noesu compounds are possibly developments from a previous w‑ or zero initial. A similar, though opposite development can be seen e.g. in the correspondence of Cantonese initial ŋ‑ and Mandarin w‑ (e.g. Cantonese ‘five’ and 1st.pro vs. Mandarin ‘five’ and 1st.pro).</par>
